revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23991619004|time triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
[[ja:伊香保温泉]][[zh:伊香保溫泉]] < {{unreferenced|date=March 2017|Sort Key=Ikaho Onsen}} {{Infobox Onsen |Name = Ikaho Onsen |image = [[File:Ikaho Onsen 03.jpg|300x300px]] |Caption = The iconic stone staircase street (2010) |Location = [[Shibukawa, Gunma|Shibukawa City]], [[Gunma Prefecture]], [[Japan]] | Latitude Degrees = 36 | Latitude Minutes = 29 | Latitude Seconds = 51 | N (North) or S (South) = N | Longitude Degrees = 138 | Longitude Minutes = 55 | Longitude Seconds = 4 | E (East) or W (West) = E |Access = [[Public Transport]]: [[Jōetsu Line]] [[Shibukawa Station]] → [[Bus|Local Bus]] (~20 min)
[[Express Bus]] "[[Jōshū Yumeguri-gō]]" (~2.5 hours from [[Shinjuku Station]])
By car: ~20 min from [[Shibukawa Ikaho Interchange]] |Spring Type = [[Sulfate Spring]] (硫酸塩泉), [[Legally Recognized Hot Spring]] (温泉法上の温泉) |Spring Temperature = |Discharge Volume = |pH Level = |Water Type Classification = |Osmotic Pressure Classification = |Number of Lodging Facilities = |Total Capacity = |Annual Bathing Visitors = |Statistics Year = |External Link = [https://www.hotels-ikaho.or.jp/ Ikaho Onsen Ryokan Cooperative Association] |Notes = }} '''Ikaho Onsen''' (伊香保温泉) is a hot spring resort located in '''Ikaho Town, Shibukawa City, Gunma Prefecture'''. It is one of the most famous onsen areas in the prefecture, alongside '''Kusatsu Onsen''', and is mentioned in the '''Jōmō Karuta''' card game as "Ikaho Onsen, a famous hot spring of Japan." The name "Ikaho Onsen" is registered as a '''Regional Collective Trademark'''.{{Sfn|JPO|2020}} == '''Spring Qualities''' == * '''Sulfate Spring''' (黄金の湯, "Golden Water") * '''Legally Recognized Hot Spring''' under the '''Hot Spring Law''' due to its metasilicic acid content (白銀の湯, "Silver Water") == '''Hot Spring Town''' == The '''onsen town''' is built along a steep incline, with '''365 stone steps''' lined by '''ryokan (Japanese inns), souvenir shops, shooting galleries, restaurants, and bathhouses'''. This iconic '''stone staircase''' is known as the "Stone Staircase Street." The '''Golden Water''' source flows from the bottom of the steps, and '''hot spring water is distributed to ryokan via wooden pipes called "Komaguchi"'''. At the top of the staircase, visitors will find '''Ikaho Shrine'''. Near the hot spring source, visitors can see '''hot spring water bubbling up''', and there is a '''drinking fountain''' along the walking path to the source. There are also '''public baths''', including '''Ikaho Outdoor hot spring baths''' (an open-air bath) and '''Ishidangai no Yu''' (a communal bath along the stone staircase). Some '''abandoned inns''' exist in the town, and in December 2023, '''Gunma Bank''' and local businesses established a company to revitalize the area, focusing on the redevelopment of unused buildings.{{Sfn|Nikkei|2024}} === '''Nearby Attractions''' === A '''ropeway (Ikaho Ropeway)''' connects the hot spring town to '''Kaminoyama Park''' on '''Mt. Monomi'''. Other notable nearby locations include: * '''Mount Haruna''' * '''Mizusawa Kannon Temple''', famous for '''Mizusawa Udon''' == '''History''' == === '''Ancient and Medieval Period''' === Ikaho is located in what was once '''Kōzuke Province'''. The '''"Jōshū-shi"''' historical record attributes its opening to '''the reign of Emperor Suinin'''.{{Sfn|National Diet Library}} The '''Man'yōshū''' poetry collection includes '''nine waka (classical Japanese poems) mentioning Ikaho'''. The '''Shintōshū''', a medieval text, recounts a '''legend''' in which '''a divine old woman moves the hot spring deeper into the mountains to prevent it from being used for washing clothes'''.{{Sfn|Kondō & Ōshima|1999}} In '''1486 (Bunmei 18)''', the '''monk Gyōe''' visited and described '''Ikaho Onsen''' in his travelogue '''"Hokkokuki"'''. The poet '''Sōgi''' also wrote in '''1502''' that Ikaho's hot springs were effective against '''palsy'''.{{Sfn|Kondō & Ōshima|1999}} During the '''Warring States Period (Sengoku Period)''', '''Nagao Kageharu''', lord of '''Shirai Castle''', managed the hot springs. '''In 1576 (Tenshō 4), Ikaho was divided among seven clans'''. Some sources say that '''Takeda Katsuyori''', after his defeat at the '''Battle of Nagashino''', ordered '''Sanada Masayuki''' to develop '''the stone staircase area to efficiently distribute hot spring water'''.{{Sfn|Gunma Prefecture|2021}} === '''Edo Period''' === From the '''Edo Period''' onward, the '''14 major families of Ikaho''' controlled the hot springs, with '''strict rules on water usage rights'''. The springs were managed using '''Komaguchi wooden water distribution ports''', and families had specific quotas for drawing water.{{Sfn|Ikaho Town Education Committee|1970}} During this period, Ikaho gained a reputation for its '''healing effects on women''', attracting '''female travelers and writers''', including: * '''Nakagawa Uchinen’s wife''' (*Ikaho Ki*) * '''Takayama Hikokurō''' (*Akagiyuki*) * '''Writers of "Ikao no Michiyukiburi" and "Kōi Nikki"* === '''Meiji and Modern Era''' === During the '''Meiji period''', '''Ikaho Onsen was visited by foreign dignitaries''', including '''Erwin von Baelz''', a German doctor who praised its medical benefits in '''"Japan's Hot Springs"'''. It was also a favorite retreat for '''literary figures''', including: * '''Tokutomi Roka''', who wrote *"Fujihototogisu"* (1898), based on his stay at '''Chiaki Ryokan''' * '''Natsume Sōseki''' * '''Hagiwara Sakutarō''' * '''Noguchi Ujō''' During the '''early 20th century''', a '''tram system (Ikaho Railway, later Tōbu Ikaho Line)''' connected Ikaho to '''Shibukawa''', but it was discontinued in '''1956'''. === '''Modern Developments''' === After '''World War II''', Ikaho also became known for '''its nightlife and entertainment, including geisha houses'''. However, the '''1990s and 2000s''' saw issues such as '''human trafficking cases''', where foreign women were forced into '''prostitution'''.{{Sfn|Yomiuri|2012}} In '''2004''', an '''onsen fraud scandal''' occurred when it was discovered that some inns were '''falsely advertising tap water as natural hot spring water'''. This was due to a '''shortage of traditional "Golden Water"''' and disputes over '''usage rights'''.{{Sfn|Gunma Prefecture|2021}} == '''Famous Local Products''' == === '''Onsen Manju (Hot Spring Steamed Buns)''' === The '''brown-colored onsen manju''', now found in hot spring towns across Japan, is said to have originated in Ikaho. '''Katsushōdō''', a local confectioner, introduced them in '''1910''', branding them '''"Sinter Manju"''' (湯の花まんじゅう).{{Sfn|Ikaho Town Education Committee|1970}} {{translated page|ja|伊香保温泉|version=104340438|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:伊香保温泉]] {{Commons category|Ikaho Onsen}} [[zh:伊香保溫泉]] {{wikidata link|Q11381013}} [[Category:Chalybeate springs]] [[Category:Geography of Shibukawa, Gunma]] [[Category:Hot springs of Gunma Prefecture]] [[Category:Regional Collective Trademarks]] [[Category:Sanada clan]] [[Category:Takeda Clan of Kai]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
